worth_it_explanation: Data retention governs how long Kevel holds the first-party user data in UserDB and what PII ends up in records and logs. A UserDB store that keeps user data with no bound, or that carries more PII than the targeting needs, raises the size of any breach and the burden of any deletion request without adding to the audit's value. The check is a review of the retention bounds and the data already written to UserDB and the shipped logs, so it costs reading and inspection time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete configuration the operator can change.
intro: Kevel is a headless, application programming interface (API) first ad server, so a publisher or retailer running an ad platform on it controls what user data is stored and for how long. Kevel keeps campaign configuration and first-party data in UserDB until the operator removes it, while transient personal data such as an internet protocol (IP) address is held only briefly during serving. This subcategory is about the retention bounds on that first-party data and the personally identifiable information (PII) that flows into UserDB records and the shipped logs. These checks confirm that user data is not kept longer than the targeting requires and that what is written to UserDB and to logs is reviewed so PII is held to the minimum the platform needs.

audit_checks.title: UserDB retention is not bounded for first-party user data
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Kevel stores UserDB records until the operator removes them, so first-party user data accumulates with no automatic expiry. Without a retention bound and a way to act on deletion requests, records persist past the point the targeting needs them, which enlarges the exposure in a breach and leaves the right-to-be-forgotten obligation unmet for users who ask to be removed.

educate.base:
UserDB is Kevel's first-party data store, where each record is tied to a userKey and holds the interest, behavior, and frequency data the operator uses for user-level targeting. Kevel keeps this first-party user data in UserDB until the operator removes it, with no automatic expiry, as Kevel's GDPR documentation describes. That design puts the retention decision on the operator rather than the platform, so a store left to run without a bound keeps user records long past the point the targeting needs them.

The cost of an unbounded store grows with time. Every record retained is data that has to be secured, may have to be disclosed in a subject-access request, and adds to the scope of any breach. A user who has not returned in a year or more still has a record carrying their interests and behavior, which serves no live targeting but remains exposed. The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and similar laws frame storage limitation as a requirement, which means holding personal data no longer than the purpose requires.

Kevel gives the operator the application programming interface (API) endpoints to act on retention. The Forget User endpoint removes a UserDB record entirely and unsets the azk cookie that carries the userKey, which is how an operator honors a right-to-be-forgotten request. Pairing that endpoint with a routine that removes records after a defined inactivity window turns retention from an open-ended default into a bounded policy.

A few signs point to UserDB retention running without a bound:
  • No defined retention window exists for UserDB records, so data is kept until someone removes it by hand
  • No routine or scheduled job calls the Forget User endpoint to clear records after a period of inactivity
  • The operator cannot describe how a deletion request from a user is fulfilled against UserDB
You may want to confirm that UserDB carries a retention bound matched to the targeting purpose and a working path for deletion requests. One approach is to set an inactivity window after which records are removed and to wire the Forget User endpoint into the deletion-request process.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site or app running a Kevel-powered ad server, UserDB often holds reader interests built up across many sessions to target house and direct-sold campaigns. You may want to set a retention window that drops records after a period of inactivity so a reader who has not returned in months is not held indefinitely, and to confirm the Forget User endpoint clears a record when a reader exercises a deletion right.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running sponsored-product ads on its own site or app through Kevel, UserDB can hold shopper behavior and interest data that feeds product targeting. You may want to bound retention to the window the targeting actually uses because a shopper's interest signal ages quickly, and to confirm a deletion request from a customer removes their UserDB record rather than leaving it in place.

investigate.intro: Review whether a retention window is defined for UserDB records and whether the Forget User endpoint is wired into a routine and into the deletion-request process.

kevel_management_api.label: Kevel Management API
steps: Read a sample of UserDB records with the Read User Record endpoint and note the age of the data each record carries.
Check whether any record reflects activity older than the targeting window the operator says it uses, which points to data kept past its purpose.
Confirm whether a scheduled job or routine calls the Forget User endpoint to remove records after a defined inactivity window.

kevel_ui.label: Kevel UI
steps: Ask the operator for the documented UserDB retention window and the process that removes records once it passes.
Walk through how a deletion request from a user is fulfilled, and confirm the path ends in a Forget User call against UserDB.
Record whether a retention bound and a deletion path both exist, or whether records are kept until removed by hand.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a retention window is defined for UserDB records, whether a routine removes records once that window passes, and whether the Forget User endpoint is wired into the deletion-request process. A setup with a documented retention bound matched to the targeting purpose and a working deletion path passes. A setup where UserDB records are kept until removed by hand, with no inactivity window and no deletion routine, is a fail recorded with the gap that was found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a UserDB retention window and a deletion path exist above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} keeps first-party user data in UserDB with no retention bound, so records persist until someone removes them by hand. Kevel stores UserDB data until the operator removes it, which means storage limitation is the operator's responsibility, and an unbounded store enlarges the exposure in a breach and leaves deletion requests harder to fulfill. {{voice_recommend}} setting an inactivity window after which UserDB records are removed and wiring the Forget User endpoint into the deletion-request process so a user can be cleared from UserDB on request. After the change, confirm that aged records are dropped and that a deletion request ends in a Forget User call.

audit_checks.title: PII written to UserDB and logs is not reviewed for minimization
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Kevel strips transient PII such as IP address and user agent within minutes of serving, but the operator decides what custom data is written to UserDB and what fields appear in the shipped logs. PII added beyond what the targeting needs, or PII that lands in retained logs, sits longer than the platform's own transient handling and widens the data an operator has to secure and disclose without improving the targeting.

educate.base:
Kevel handles two kinds of personal data differently. Transient personal data such as an internet protocol (IP) address, a user agent string, and latitude or longitude is held only for a few minutes while a decision is served and post-processed, then dropped, as Kevel's GDPR documentation describes. The first-party data in UserDB is different because the operator chooses what to write to each record and Kevel keeps it until the operator removes it. The audit question sits on the data the operator controls, which is what lands in UserDB and what appears in the shipped logs.

The risk is writing more personally identifiable information (PII) than the targeting requires. UserDB is built for interest, behavior, and frequency signals tied to a userKey, not for a name, an email address, or a raw identifier that points back to a person. When fields like those are written into a custom property on a record, they become first-party data the platform keeps with no automatic expiry, which raises the breach scope and the disclosure burden without making the targeting work any better. Kevel's data processing agreement also names data it will not store, including health information and payment card information, which is a floor the operator should not cross.

The shipped logs carry the second surface. Kevel can ship raw event logs to an operator's own storage, and those logs can include the fields the operator chose to pass on the decision request. A field added for one report can persist in retained logs far longer than the few minutes Kevel holds transient PII during serving, so a review of what the logs carry is part of keeping PII to the minimum.

A few signs point to PII that has not been reviewed for minimization:
  • UserDB records carry custom properties holding a name, an email address, or a raw identifier rather than interest and behavior signals
  • Shipped logs include personal fields passed on the decision request that no report actually uses
  • No review exists comparing the data written to UserDB and the logs against the data the targeting and reporting need
You may want to confirm that what is written to UserDB and to the logs is held to the data the targeting and reporting require. One approach is to read a sample of UserDB records and a slice of the shipped logs, then remove any personal field that no use depends on.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site or app running a Kevel-powered ad server, a custom property is sometimes used to carry a subscriber identifier or contact field into UserDB for convenience. You may want to confirm those records hold interest and behavior signals rather than direct identifiers, and that the shipped logs do not retain a personal field the reporting never reads.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running sponsored-product ads on its own site or app through Kevel, a UserDB record or a log field can pick up a customer email or account identifier passed from the storefront. You may want to confirm that the data feeding sponsored-product targeting is interest and behavior rather than direct PII, and that any personal field in the shipped logs is removed if no report depends on it.

investigate.intro: Review what custom data is written to UserDB records and what personal fields appear in the shipped logs, then compare them against the data the targeting and reporting actually use.

kevel_management_api.label: Kevel Management API
steps: Read a sample of UserDB records with the Read User Record endpoint and list the custom properties each record carries.
Flag any property holding a name, an email address, or a raw identifier rather than an interest, behavior, or frequency signal.
Confirm that no record carries data the data processing agreement names as off limits, such as health information or payment card information.

kevel_decision_api.label: Kevel Decision API
steps: Inspect a sample of the raw event logs Kevel ships to the operator's own storage and list the fields each record contains.
Flag any personal field passed on the decision request, then check whether a report actually reads it.
Record which personal fields in UserDB and the logs are not used by any targeting or report, which points to PII that minimization would remove.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the custom data written to UserDB records and the personal fields present in the shipped logs, then compares both against the data the targeting and reporting depend on. A setup where UserDB holds interest and behavior signals, the logs carry only fields a report uses, and no off-limits data appears passes. A setup where records or logs carry a name, an email address, a raw identifier, or other PII that no use depends on is a fail recorded with the fields that minimization would remove.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the custom data in UserDB and the personal fields in the shipped logs above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} writes personally identifiable information (PII) to UserDB and the shipped logs that the targeting and reporting do not use. Kevel drops transient data such as an internet protocol (IP) address within minutes of serving, but the operator controls what lands in UserDB and the logs, so a name, an email address, or a raw identifier written there is kept with no expiry and widens the data to secure and disclose. {{voice_recommend}} reading a sample of UserDB records and a slice of the shipped logs, then removing any personal field that no targeting or report depends on so the stored data holds to the minimum the platform needs. After the change, recheck the records and logs to confirm the personal fields are gone.
